intro:
The condescension of God is His gracious stooping to the level of His creatures — accommodating His infinite majesty to finite, fallen human capacity in order to reveal Himself, to enter covenant, and to save. The term does not mean that God surrenders His glory or changes His nature; rather, the eternal, self-sufficient God freely lowers Himself to meet creatures who could never ascend to Him. As the Psalmist marvels, “Who is like the LORD our God, who is seated on high, who looks far down on the heavens and the earth?” (Psalm 113:5–6). His highness and His nearness are not in tension: He is exalted precisely as the God who draws near to the lowly.

Scripture displays this condescension from its opening pages. The LORD walks in the garden in the cool of the day (Genesis 3:8); He “comes down” to see the tower at Babel (Genesis 11:5) and to deliver His people from Egypt: “I have come down to deliver them” (Exodus 3:8). He descends upon Sinai in fire and cloud (Exodus 19:18), and then condescends to dwell among Israel: “Let them make me a sanctuary, that I may dwell in their midst” (Exodus 25:8). The prophets hold together God’s transcendence and His tender nearness: “Thus says the One who is high and lifted up, who inhabits eternity… ‘I dwell in the high and holy place, and also with him who is of a contrite and lowly spirit’” (Isaiah 57:15). “Though the LORD is high, he regards the lowly” (Psalm 138:6).

God’s very self-revelation is an act of condescension. He speaks human words, takes human names, and stoops to anthropomorphic language — describing Himself with hands, eyes, and a voice — so that creatures might know Him truly, though never exhaustively. He accommodates Himself to His people as a father carries his child (Deuteronomy 1:31) and as a parent who taught Ephraim to walk (Hosea 11:3–4). All true knowledge of God rests on this gracious stooping: we know Him not by climbing up to His heights but because He has bent down to ours.

The condescension of God reaches its summit in the incarnation of the Son. The eternal Word “became flesh and dwelt among us” (John 1:14), and Paul sets it forth as the pattern of all humility: though He was “in the form of God,” Christ “emptied himself, by taking the form of a servant… and humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross” (Philippians 2:6–8). “Though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich” (2 Corinthians 8:9). The God who came down to Babel and Sinai came down at last in person — laid in a manger, washing His disciples’ feet (John 13:4–5), and bearing the curse of sinners. Here divine condescension and divine love are one.

This truth guards both the majesty and the mercy of God. It humbles human pride: if the Most High stoops, His people must clothe themselves in humility toward one another (Philippians 2:3–5). It comforts the lowly: the high and holy God makes His home with the contrite. And it fuels worship, for every act of grace — creation, covenant, incarnation, the Spirit’s indwelling, and the final descent of the holy city when “the dwelling place of God is with man” (Revelation 21:3) — is God graciously coming down to us. The whole gospel is the story of the God who, without ceasing to be God, draws near.
